era_context: Sargon II's Neo-Assyrian Empire is at the height of its territorial extent and administrative sophistication. The empire stretches from Egypt to Mesopotamia to Anatolia. In China, the Spring and Autumn Period continues with regional states consolidating power. Greece is in the Archaic Period, with city-states developing democratic and oligarchic institutions.

events.title: Sargon II's continued consolidation of Assyrian Empire and potential campaign in the north
events.description: Sargon II continues military and administrative consolidation of the Assyrian Empire. Campaigns in the north, possibly toward the Caucasus and beyond, are undertaken to secure borders and suppress potential threats. The empire's administrative system is refined, with improved communication networks and military logistics. These efforts aim to maintain control over the vast, ethnically diverse empire and prevent rebellions among subject peoples.
events.certainty_note: Specific events for 715 BCE are not clearly distinguished in surviving sources. This entry reflects the broader context of Sargon II's reign. Chronological uncertainty is ±2–3 years.
cross_references: Part of broader Assyrian expansion -745 to -705

events.title: Possible early Greek colonial expansion in the Mediterranean
events.description: During the Archaic Period, Greek city-states are establishing colonies throughout the Mediterranean and Black Sea regions. By 715 BCE, colonies have been established in southern Italy, Sicily, and the Black Sea coast. These colonies serve as trading posts, outlets for population pressure, and sources of raw materials. The colonial movement is part of the broader development of the Greek polis system and the expansion of Greek cultural and economic influence.

sources.name: Later Greek historians such as Herodotus and Strabo

events.certainty_note: The dating of early Greek colonies is based on archaeological evidence (pottery, stratigraphy) and is often uncertain by ±10–20 years. Specific events for 715 BCE are not well-attested.
cross_references: Part of broader Greek colonial expansion 750–550 BCE

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of early Greek colonization is highly debated. Archaeological dating of early colonial sites is uncertain, and the traditional dates given by later Greek historians (Herodotus, Strabo) are often questioned by modern scholars. Some colonies may have been established earlier or later than traditionally dated. The extent of Sargon II's northern campaigns is also debated; some scholars argue that his power did not extend as far north as inscriptions suggest.
historiographic_note: For 715 BCE, documentation is sparse and heavily skewed toward Mesopotamia and the Near East. Greek sources are entirely from much later periods and may not accurately reflect events of this specific year. Chinese sources are unreliable. Most of the world has no written records. The historical record for this year is fragmentary and dependent on inscriptional evidence and archaeology, both of which have significant dating uncertainties.

graph_edges.note: Sargon II's consolidation continues until his death in 705 BCE
Greek colonial expansion continues through the Archaic Period
